coredata.dc:title: Xylooligosaccharides, monosaccharides, and pH-sensitive carbon dots production from Toona sinensis branches using organic acid hydrolysis and hydrothermal treatment

coredata.dc:description: The present study focused on exploring the effectiveness of delignification of the lignocellulosic biomass and pH-controlled organic acid hydrolysis in the cascade utilization of Toona sinensis branches (TB) for the production of xylooligosaccharides (XOS), monosaccharides and carbon dots (CDs). The hydrolysis of delignified TB with propionic acid (PA) resulted in a high XOS yield of 48.1 % at pH 3.0, 170 °C for 60 min. The PA hydrolyzates upon hydrolysis with xylanase yielded 61.2 % XOS. The solid residue from XOS production was subjected to cellulase hydrolysis, resulting in a glucose yield of 87.8 %. Furthermore, CDs were synthesized through a green hydrothermal method using the solid residue from cellulase hydrolysis as a precursor. These CDs exhibited excitation-independent and pH-dependent fluorescence properties. This study demonstrated the integrated utilization of TB for efficient production of XOS, monosaccharides, and pH-sensitive CDs.

head.ce:title: Xylooligosaccharides, monosaccharides, and pH-sensitive carbon dots production from Toona sinensis branches using organic acid hydrolysis and hydrothermal treatment

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The present study focused on exploring the effectiveness of delignification of the lignocellulosic biomass and pH-controlled organic acid hydrolysis in the cascade utilization of Toona sinensis branches (TB) for the production of xylooligosaccharides (XOS), monosaccharides and carbon dots (CDs). The hydrolysis of delignified TB with propionic acid (PA) resulted in a high XOS yield of 48.1 % at pH 3.0, 170 °C for 60 min. The PA hydrolyzates upon hydrolysis with xylanase yielded 61.2 % XOS. The solid residue from XOS production was subjected to cellulase hydrolysis, resulting in a glucose yield of 87.8 %. Furthermore, CDs were synthesized through a green hydrothermal method using the solid residue from cellulase hydrolysis as a precursor. These CDs exhibited excitation-independent and pH-dependent fluorescence properties. This study demonstrated the integrated utilization of TB for efficient production of XOS, monosaccharides, and pH-sensitive CDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The production of high-value products from lignocellulosic biomass, a renewable and abundant resource, is crucial for the sustainable development of biorefineries [1,2]. Xylooligosaccharides (XOS), with a degree of polymerization (DP) ranging from 2 to 6, are derived from hemicellulose through acid or enzymatic hydrolysis and have been explored for their prebiotic potential in promoting gut health of humans and animals, even at low doses [3–5]. Toona sinensis, a shrub of the Meliaceae family in Asia, is renowned for its nutritious tender buds [6]. Nevertheless, a considerable quantity of lignified tissue from Toona sinensis (e.g., Toona sinensis branches) is discarded or burned, resulting in the underutilization of this agroforestry resource, contributing to environmental pollution. The utilization of Toona sinensis branches (TB) for XOS production supports a circular economy by converting waste biomass into valuable products, thereby reducing reliance on fossil-based materials. This process not only mitigates environmental pollution caused by agricultural residues but also decreases the carbon footprint associated with traditional waste disposal methods such as burning or landfilling [7].

The simple and rapid method for the XOS production from hemicellulosic xylan providing high yield is organic acid hydrolysis [1,2,8]. However, xylan in hemicellulose is tightly bound to lignin, limiting its accessibility to the chemical reagents, [9] thereby impeding efficient XOS production. Delignification has been demonstrated to markedly enhance XOS production during the organic acid hydrolysis of lignocelluloses [8]. In our previous experiment, XOS yields of 33.1 % and 38.7 % are obtained from raw untreated TB by hydrolysis using 2 % lactic acid (LA) and 6 % propionic acid (PA) at 170 °C for 60 min, respectively [7]. Nevertheless, the implications of delignification on XOS production from TB using organic acid hydrolysis remain uncertain and need to be elucidated.

Additionally, it has been reported that the pH of organic acid solution affects XOS production from hemicellulose [2]. Lower pH environments, with higher proton concentrations, accelerate carbohydrate degradation [10]. However, excessively low pH can reduce XOS yield. Proper pH adjustments during hydrolysis, controlling excessive acidity by neutralizing the released acid and preventing the formation of by-products like furfural formation while enhancing hemicellulose release for better XOS production [11–13]. For instance, raising the pH value of a 2 % lactic acid solution from 2.0 to 2.5 can increase the XOS yield of delignified poplar by 20.2 % [2]. A previous study has demonstrated successful production of XOS by hydrolysis of TB using 2 % LA or 6 % PA solutions [7]. However, the effects of pH values of LA or PA solutions on the formation of XOS from TB are not clear. Thus, exploring the effects of pH adjustment of PA and LA solutions during hydrolytic production of XOS from TB is of great interest.

Carbon dots (CDs), as quasi-zero-dimensional fluorescent carbon nanomaterials, have garnered significant attention for their unique properties, including small size, low biotoxicity, and tunable photoluminescence [14,15]. A wide range of green, inexpensive, and readily available natural compounds (e.g. lignin and polysaccharides) exhibits active and complex chemistries resembling CDs [16]. The structure of lignin in particular is comparable to that of CDs, as both include several aromatic rings and oxygen-containing functional groups. Thus, lignocellulosic materials are an ideal precursor for the synthesis of CDs [16,17]. The efficient utilization of the main components of the solid residue after the production of XOS and glucose from TB is challenging. Hydrolysis residues are typically used for direct combustion to provide energy for biorefinery systems. This, however, undoubtedly results in a huge waste of resources and environmental pollution [15,18]. Therefore, using solid residues of TB hydrolysis for the production of CDs could maximize the value of these residues. Recent studies focus on the synthesis process and photoluminescence mechanism of CDs synthesized with a single component as the precursor [3,5]. Comprehensive research on synthesizing CDs from hydrolysis residues remains largely unexplored. Thus, it is necessary to explore the feasibility of improving the comprehensive utilization of all components of TB for CDs production from solid residues of TB hydrolysis.

Building on previous studies that investigated organic acid (PA or LA) hydrolysis of TB for XOS production [7], this study aimed to fill two critical gaps: (1) the unexplored effects of TB delignification and pH adjustments of acid solutions on XOS yields, and (2) the underutilization of hydrolysis residues for the synthesis of value-added products, such as carbon dots (CDs). The effects of pH variations of PA and LA solutions on XOS production from raw and delignified TB were systematically examined to accomplish these goals. High-DP XOS in the hydrolysates was further hydrolyzed into low-DP XOS (DP 2–6) using xylanase to enhance yield. The hydrolysis residue was subsequently treated with cellulase to produce glucose, and the final solid residue was utilized as a precursor for synthesizing photoluminescent CDs via a hydrothermal method. The synthesized CDs were comprehensively characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM), high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy to assess their structural and optical properties. Furthermore, the pH sensing capability of the CDs in aqueous solution was evaluated to explore their potential as biosensors. These innovations not only improve the efficiency of TB utilization for the integrated production of XOS, glucose, and CDs but also create opportunities for their practical industrial applications. Specifically, XOS can be used as food and feed additives, glucose is used in ethanol and organic acid fermentation, and CDs can be applied as fluorescent probes and biosensors, contributing to sustainable biomass utilization in biorefinery processes.

Toona sinensis branches were obtained from Ziyang, Shaanxi Province, China, and sieved through a 60-mesh screen (≤0.25 mm). The enzymes used for hydrolysis experiments, namely cellulase (Celtic CTec2) and xylanase (X2753, Pentopan Mono BG) having enzyme activities of 123 FPU/mL and 100 U/mL, respectively, were obtained from Sigma (St. Louis, USA).

A hydrogen peroxide-acetic acid (HPAA) solution was prepared by mixing equal volumes of 30 % hydrogen peroxide and 99 % acetic acid. The HPAA solution was then diluted to 80 % with deionized water [7]. Untreated TB (1.0 g) was mixed with the 80 % HPAA solution and heated at 60 °C for 2 h (solid-liquid ratio of 1:10), with 100 mM H2SO4 as a catalyst for the delignification of TB. The resulting solid residue (delignified TB) was thoroughly washed until neutral, dried, and utilized in subsequent acid hydrolysis experiments.

The calculations to determine the recoveries of solid, glucan, and xylan, as well as the degree of delignification of TB after HPAA treatment were carried out using the following equations:

ce:section.ce:section-title: Organic acid hydrolysis for XOS production
ce:section.ce:para:
The TB or delignified TB (1.0 g) was mixed with 2 % (w/v) LA or 6 % (w/v) PA solution in a reactor and the reaction was carried out under optimum conditions of 170 °C for 60 min (Table 1 ) [7]. After the reaction, the LA or PA hydrolyzates were collected for the determination of XOS, glucose, and xylose concentrations. The hydrolysis experiments were performed in triplicate. The XOS yield (DP 2–6) and the ratio of xylose to XOS were carried out using the following equations:

The LA or PA hydrolyzates were digested with xylanase with concentrations of 1.5, 3.0, or 5.0 mg protein/g xylan in sodium citrate buffer (pH 4.5) at 50 °C for 12 h to convert XOS with high DP -(DP > 6) in the hydrolyzates into XOS with low DP (DP 2–6) [2]. The XOS (DP 2–6) yield and the ratio of xylose to XOS were calculated using the formulas in eqs. (4) and (5).

To liberate the monosaccharides trapped in the filtered solid residue after XOS production via xylanase hydrolysis, cellulase hydrolysis was performed at a solid loading of 2 % (w/v) in sodium citrate buffer (pH 4.8) with an enzyme dosage of 5–10 FPU cellulase/g dry mass (DM) at a temperature of 50 °C for 72 h [19,20]. The glucose and xylose concentrations after cellulase hydrolysis were determined by high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC). The glucose and xylose yields were determined using the formulas in eqs. (6) and (7).

The National Renewable Energy Laboratory (NREL) standard procedures were followed while determining the chemical compositions of TB samples [21]. Glucose and xylose concentrations in the hydrolyzates from organic acid and enzymatic hydrolysis were measured by HPLC using an Agilent 1200 instrument. Using high-performance anion-exchange chromatography, the concentration of XOS in the hydrolyzates was determined [2,7].

CDs were prepared using a single-step hydrothermal method that employed ammonium citrate as a precursor [22]. A solution of ammonium citrate (2.0 g) in water (60 g) was prepared, and the solid residue resulting from cellulase hydrolysis was added to this solution. The mixture was subjected to sonication for 15 min to ensure adequate dispersion. It was then transferred to an autoclave (LICHEN, China) where the hydrothermal reaction was conducted at 200 °C for 1.5 h. After the reaction, the reaction mixture was cooled to room temperature and subjected to centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 10 min to separate the solid and liquid phases. The aqueous solution was then dialyzed in deionized water using a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cutoff of 1000 Da, with the water being refreshed every 6 h, to separate the CDs. The resulting yellow solution was then concentrated by spin evaporation and freeze-dried subsequently, yielding the CDs.

The surface functional groups of CDs were characterized by XPS using an AXIS Ultra DLD photoelectron spectrometer (Kratos, Japan). FTIR (Nicolet Nexus 470, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) was conducted over 400 to 4000 cm−1 wavelength range. The particle size and morphology of the CDs in aqueous solution were examined using TEM and HR-TEM (Talos F200i TEM, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). For imaging, the aqueous solution containing CDs was deposited onto a clean copper grid and allowed to dry naturally, to ensure an even distribution of the CDs. UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded with a DU 800 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Beckman, USA), and fluorescence spectroscopy was conducted using an Edinburgh FLS980 spectrophotometer (Edinburgh, UK).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of pH values of organic acids on XOS production
Production of XOS from raw, untreated TB
ce:section.ce:para: The optimal hydrolysis conditions for TB using 2 % LA or 6 % PA as reported in the previous studies, were 170 °C for 60 min [7]. However, the effect of pH adjustment on XOS yield has not been fully explored. The glycosidic bond-breaking process of hemicellulose during acid hydrolysis reactions is stochastic. During the conversion of hemicellulose to XOS via acid hydrolysis, the extent of hemicellulose breakdown must be controlled to avoid excessive degradation of hemicellulose [11]. To investigate the effects of pH values of LA or PA solutions on XOS production from TB, 2 % LA and 6 % PA solutions with various pH values (2.0, 2.5, 3.0, 3.5, and 4.0) were prepared by adding appropriate quantities of 20 % (w/w) NaOH or 10 % (w/w) HCl aqueous solution to the acid solutions. As shown in Fig. 1a, a high XOS yield of 35.2 % was obtained with LA solution at pH 2.5. The XOS yields decreased to 22.4 % and 13.2 % when the LA solutions of pH 3.0 and 4.0 were used, respectively. These trends were similar to those observed in the pH-controlled acetic acid pretreatment for XOS from corncobs and LA hydrolysis of poplar [2,12]. Similarly, a 40.1 % XOS yield was obtained by adjusting the pH of the PA solution to 3.0 as shown in Fig. 1b. Compared to previous experiments using 2 % LA (pH = 2.0) and 6 % PA (pH = 2.5) without pH adjustment [7], the XOS yields of TB hydrolysis with the pH-adjusted LA (pH = 2.5) and PA (pH = 3.0) solutions showed improvements of 6.3 % and 3.6 %, respectively. The ratio of xylose to XOS in the hydrolyzates serves as an indicator of the relative purity of XOS, and the value of this ratio is inversely proportional to the relative purity of XOS [2]. When the pH of the PA solution was below 3.0, a negative correlation was observed between xylose yield and pH, while a positive correlation existed between the relative purity of XOS and pH. At pH 3.0, the highest XOS purity was achieved. However, when the pH of the PA solution exceeded 3.0, the XOS yield decreased significantly. Notably, the relative purity of PA hydrolyzates consistently remained higher than those of LA hydrolyzates (Fig. 1a and b), indicating that PA hydrolysis of TB was more suitable for producing highly pure XOS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Production of XOS from delignified TB
ce:section.ce:para: Previous studies have confirmed that delignification treatment of biomass enhances XOS production during acid hydrolysis [2,23]. To improve the efficiency of the conversion of xylan in delignified TB into XOS, XOS production was evaluated using LA and PA solutions of varied pH values. As shown in Fig. 1c, an XOS yield of 41.4 % was obtained using an LA solution of pH 2.5, representing an 8.3 % increase compared to that obtained with the LA solution of pH 2.0 (control). When the pH exceeded 2.5, the XOS yield decreased with increasing pH, dropping to 25.2 % at pH 3.0 and 14.4 % at pH 4.0. Similarly, when the pH of the PA solution was adjusted to 3.0, the XOS yield was as high as 48.1 %, higher than 44.7 % at pH 2.5 (control) (p < 0.05) (Fig. 1d). These findings suggested that after the delignification of TB and pH adjustment of the organic acid solution, XOS yields using LA (pH = 2.5) and PA (pH = 3.0) improved significantly, reaching 41.4 % and 48.1 %, respectively. Compared to the XOS yields reported by Lin et al. [7], the yields in this study were increased by a factor of 1.25 for LA and 1.24 for PA.

Lignin is widely recognized as a major barrier to the efficient conversion of hemicellulose to high-value XOS [23]. After highly selective delignification of TB by HPAA, a significant quantity of carbohydrates was retained in the delignified TB (Table 2 ). Due to the delignification, the reaction sites of xylan became more accessible to LA or PA, facilitating easier hydrolysis, ultimately increasing XOS yields [2]. During acid hydrolysis of hemicellulose, the acid first dissociates in water to generate hydrogen ions which protonate the water molecules to form hydronium ions (H3O+). The hydronium ions rapidly protonate the glycosidic oxygen atoms of hemicellulose macromolecules to form conjugate acid. This weakens the glycosidic bond energy and fracture facilitates bond cleavage, ultimately generating monosaccharides [24,25]. During acid hydrolysis, continuous depolymerization of hemicellulose occurs, resulting in the formation of oligosaccharides with different degrees of polymerization. These oligosaccharides undergo further hydrolysis forming monosaccharides [10]. The pH adjustment of the LA and PA solutions during hydrolysis revealed that as the pH was increased from 2.0 to 4.0, the XOS yield was increased initially and then gradually decreased (Fig. 1). This phenomenon was primarily attributed to the lower pH, which increased the concentration of H+ ions, thereby enhancing their ability to protonate and weaken glycosidic bonds in xylan, leading to more efficient cleavage. The increased acidity, however, also caused excessive breakdown of xylan, resulting in higher xylose yields and lower XOS production [2,8,23]. The buffering action of NaOH in the acid solution stabilized the proton concentration and decreased the rate of xylan hydrolysis [11,12]. In summary, a combination of delignification and pH-controlled hydrolysis of xylan using PA or LA resulted in high XOS yields of 41.4 % and 48.1 % from delignified TB in LA (pH 2.5) and PA (pH 3.0) hydrolyzates, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Xylanase hydrolysis of high DP XOS in hydrolyzates
ce:section.ce:para: After the acid hydrolysis of lignocellulose, some high-DP XOS might remain in the hydrolyzates. This XOS can be further hydrolyzed to low-DP XOS (DP 2–6) using xylanase [26]. Previous studies on LA hydrolyzates derived from poplar showed that continuous hydrolysis with xylanase at doses of 5–20 IU/g xylan resulted in enhancements in XOS yield to varying degrees, with both xylanase loading and hydrolysis time largely affecting the yield [2,11]. To explore the effects of xylanase loading and hydrolysis time on the production of low-DP XOS in LA and PA hydrolyzates, they were subjected to xylanase (1.5–5.0 mg protein/g xylan) hydrolysis over 0–12 h.

As seen in Fig. 2a, when a low dose of xylanase (1.5 mg/g xylan) was used to hydrolyze LA hydrolyzates (pH = 2.5) for 4 h, the XOS yield was 56.3 %. XOS yields of LA hydrolyzates were increased to 56.2 % and 57.2 % after 2 h using xylanase dosages of 3.0 and 5.0 mg/g xylan, respectively. Thus, the optimal conditions for hydrolyzing the LA hydrolyzates were achieved using a low dose of xylanase (1.5 mg/g xylan) for 4 h For PA hydrolyzates (pH = 3.0) (Fig. 2b), very high XOS yields of 61.2 % and 62.5 % were achieved with xylanase dosages of 3.0 and 5.0 mg/g xylan after 2 h. Therefore, hydrolyzing PA hydrolyzates with 3.0 mg/g xylan for 2 h was more appropriate to convert high-DP XOS into low-DP XOS (DP 2–6).

These results confirmed that the delignification of TB and regulation of PA solution pH played an important role in producing low-DP XOS, which is more effective as a prebiotic in the food and feed industries for inducing intestinal bacteria than high-DP XOS [27–29]. In the PA or LA hydrolysis of different TB samples, by-products such as monosaccharides, formic acid, acetic acid, and furfural were also generated in the hydrolyzates (Table 3 ). These are mainly formed by intramolecular dehydration and cyclization of xylose produced by acid hydrolysis in the presence of hydrogen ions [24]. In food and feed applications, the prebiotic effect of XOS depends on its purity level, and therefore the purification of XOS is highly desirable [30]. In the last decades, several purification techniques such as membrane filtration, chromatographic, and adsorption-separation technologies have been used for XOS purification in TB hydrolyzates [31].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellulase hydrolysis of TB solid residue for glucose production
ce:section.ce:para: Following sequential hydrolysis of TB for XOS production using organic acid and xylanase, the cellulose in the delignified TB-LA2.5 and delignified TB-PA3.0 solid residues was converted to glucose through cellulase hydrolysis, with TB, TB-LA2.5, and TB-PA3.0 samples serving as the controls. Previous study has demonstrated that the monosaccharide yields from treated corncobs could be enhanced by increasing the cellulase dosage from 5 to 10 FPU cellulase/g DM [32]. To achieve a balance between enzymatic efficiency and cost-effectiveness, enzyme loading levels were carefully optimized in this study. Cellulase hydrolysis of untreated TB at a dosage of 5 FPU/g DM provided glucose and xylose in only 4.4 % and 2.1 % yields, respectively (Fig. 3a). These low yields highlighted the need for pretreatment of the raw material to enhance enzyme accessibility. The glucose and xylose yield from delignified TB-LA2.5 were 66.3 % and 45.2 %, respectively, representing 15- and 22-fold increases compared to those obtained from the untreated raw materials. At a cellulase dosage of 8 FPU/g DM (Fig. 3b), the glucose yields of delignified TB-LA2.5 and delignified TB-PA3.0 were 90.0 % and 87.8 %, respectively, which were 1.4 times higher than those obtained with 5 FPU/g DM. However, raising the cellulase dosage to 10 FPU/g DM (Fig. 3c) provided only marginal improvements, with glucose yields reaching 92.3 % and 90.8 % for delignified TB-LA2.5 and delignified TB-PA3.0, respectively. Given the minimal gains in the yields relative to the additional enzyme cost, 8 FPU/g DM was selected as the optimal cellulase loading level. These findings underscored the importance of optimizing enzyme dosage to maximize hydrolysis efficiency while maintaining economic feasibility, a key consideration for scaling up biorefinery processes.

The mass balance of producing XOS and monosaccharides from Toona sinensis branches (TB) following PA and enzymatic hydrolysis is shown in Fig. 4 . Glucan, xylan, and lignin (459.0 g, 166.0 g, and 281.0 g, respectively) were the main components of raw TB. This raw TB ultimately yielded 89.6 g of XOS after subjecting to sequential delignification by HPAA solution, PA hydrolysis (6 % w/v PA at 170 °C for 60 min), and xylanase hydrolysis (50 °C for 2 h). A solid residue of delignified TB-PA3.0 (684.0 g) from the production of XOS was hydrolyzed by cellulase to yield 289.4 g of glucose and 17.9 g xylose. After subjecting TB to PA and enzymatic hydrolysis to produce XOS and monosaccharides, a final solid residue consisting of 45.7 % glucan, 14.3 % xylan, and 25.9 % lignin was obtained. The effective utilization of the final solid residue is crucial to achieving comprehensive use of the whole biomass components. Therefore, it is necessary to explore the feasibility of using TB solid residue as a raw material for producing high-value-added products.

ce:section.ce:section-title: TB solid residue for CDs production

ce:section.ce:para: The most efficient way to utilize the lignocellulosic biomass is through cascade utilization, which comprises the processing of the biomass based on its composition and characteristics [4,33]. The TB residue still contained glucan, xylan, and lignin after the production of XOS and monosaccharides (Fig. 4). The abundance of sp 2 hybridized benzene ring structures in lignin makes it an ideal precursor for the assembly of carbon dots (CDs) [3,34]. The xylan and glucan in the TB solid residue are rich in hydroxyl groups, which facilitate the production of advanced photoluminescent CDs after chemical modifications [3]. CDs with blue fluorescence were synthesized from the TB solid residue as a precursor and ammonium citrate as the nitrogen source through a simple one-step hydrothermal method. As shown in Fig. 5a, the CDs in aqueous solution appeared yellowish-brown under normal light, and rapidly dissolved in water without any visible undissolved particles, demonstrating their excellent water solubility, a characteristic feature of CDs. Fig. 5b indicated that the CDs in aqueous solution displayed blue fluorescence when exposed to a wavelength of 365 nm.

Employing different precursors and synthesis processes, CDs with various morphologies can be obtained [35]. However, due to the similarity in chemical composition and functional groups, distinguishing between different types of CDs can be challenging. Therefore, TEM and HRTEM are commonly employed to analyze the morphology and crystal structure of lignin-derived CDs [3]. The formation of CDs was confirmed through TEM, as shown in Fig. 5c. The image exhibits a uniform dispersion of nanoparticles within the aqueous solution. The particle size distribution of the CDs, determined through Gaussian fitting, revealed the average particle size of 4.7 nm. The crystalline nature of the CDs was further validated by HRTEM measurements (Fig. 5d). The analysis depicted a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm that closely aligned with the (1,0,0) crystal plane spacing of graphite [36].

FT-IR was used to elucidate the functional groups of CDs, and XPS was used to determine the C, O, and N contents and the composition of functional groups on the surface of CDs [37]. The FTIR spectra (Fig. 6a), showed the CN stretching vibration peaks in the range from 1037 to 1516 cm−1 [5]. The XPS spectra of the CDs are presented in Fig. 6b, where the C 1 s, O 1 s, and N 1 s signals are represented by the peaks at 285.6, 541.6, and 396.8 eV, respectively. These findings confirmed the successful incorporation of nitrogen into the CDs using ammonium citrate precursor. The CDs were composed of 16.53 % nitrogen, 18.71 % oxygen, and 65.8 % carbon [38]. The peaks due to various functional groups such as C–C/C=C at 283.9 eV, CN at 281.5 eV, and CO at 279.4 eV could be seen in Fig. 6c. Fig. 6d shows the O 1 s spectrum, with distinct peaks at 533.2, 531.7, and 530.5 eV, corresponding to CO (69.6 %), C–OH (21.5 %), and C–O–C (8.9 %), respectively. The N 1 s spectra (Fig. 6e) displayed two peaks at 400.5 and 399.1 eV corresponding to pyrrolic NH (14.5 %) and pyridinic CN (85.5 %), respectively [39]. These results suggested that the CN bonds were more prevalent than NH bonds in the CDs, indicating that nitrogen was mostly incorporated in the CN bonds. The XPS confirmed the existence of hydrophilic functional groups on the surface of the CDs. The CDs synthesized by hydrothermal treatment demonstrated excellent water solubility. Chemically, the carbon core of the CDs exhibited a graphitic structure, which was inherently hydrophobic, while the surface of the CDs contained hydrophilic oxygen-containing functional groups [3]. At elevated temperatures, water undergoes enhanced ionization, creating a mildly acidic system that depolymerizes hemicellulose into monosaccharides, including hexoses and pentoses. These hydrolysis products subsequently undergo dehydration, rearrangement, and fragmentation reactions, forming intermediates (e.g., furfural and 5-Hydroxymethylfurfural) [40,41]. As shown in Fig. 7 , the polymerization of these intermediates generates stable aromatic clusters [42,43]. Similarly, lignin depolymerizes into aromatic acids and phenols, which further condense via aldol reactions and cycloadditions forming stable aromatic frameworks [3,44]. Dopant molecules play a critical role in promoting condensation, either by integrating into the carbon cores or accumulating on their surfaces, ultimately leading to the formation of spherical carbon nanospheres [3].

Surface passivation or functionalization can alter the UV–vis absorption properties of CDs, causing a red shift in the absorption wavelength [45]. The UV–Vis absorption spectra of CDs (Fig. 8a) revealed a peak at 280 nm, attributed to the π → π* transition of the CC bond. The increased presence of certain chemical groups attached to the aromatic rings created a physical hindrance to the free movement of the electrons within the CDs [46]. Furthermore, an additional absorption peak was detected at 350 nm, attributed to the n → π* transition between oxygen or nitrogen-containing groups and sp 2 domains [17,47]. Specifically, the nitrogen heteroatoms triggered the formation of excited defect surface states, which resulted in this peak [48,49].

Photoluminescence is one of the most distinctive and fascinating optical properties of CDs [45]. CDs are composed of carbon cores and organic molecules, with surface functional groups covalently attached to the carbon core. This particular composition imparts CDs with remarkable fluorescence and semiconductor properties [3]. As shown in Fig. 8b, within the excitation wavelength range of 300 to 400 nm, the emission wavelength range of CDs was mainly concentrated between 350 and 600 nm, displaying high uniformity. The emission peak position of CDs remained stable around 425 nm. However, with an increase in the excitation wavelength from 360 to 400 nm the fluorescence intensity of CDs decreased noticeably. This excitation-independent fluorescence emission behavior in CDs was primarily attributed to the presence of similar functional groups, with defects effectively passivated [50,51].

The pH sensing capability of the CDs in dilute aqueous solution was evaluated across a wide pH range (1.0–12.0) by adjusting the CDs solution pH with HCl or NaOH. Under 360 nm excitation, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs diminished significantly in highly strongly basic conditions (pH ≥ 10) (Fig. 8c). Within the pH range of 1.0 to 9.0, the fluorescence intensity exhibited a marked increase with rising pH, reaching a maximum at pH 9.0. As shown in Fig. 8d, the linear fitting equation exhibited a high correlation coefficient (R2 = 0.96), likely attributed to the protonation and deprotonation of surface functional groups (e.g., -COOH and -NH2) on CDs. At high pH, the deprotonation of -COOH groups to -COO−, along with the retention of deprotonated -NH2 groups, induces electrostatic doping. This doping shifts the Fermi level toward lower energy states, altering the electronic band structure and suppressing radiative transitions [36,52]. Concurrently, deprotonation passivated surface defects by generating a dense, negatively charged “protective shell” of -COO− groups. This shell not only isolated individual CDs but also reduced electron-hole trapping at defect sites [53,54]. Furthermore, at elevated pH, the enhanced surface negative charge triggered weak noncovalent interactions such as hydrogen bonding between adjacent CDs. These interactions led to structural rigidity and aggregation. They also diverted the absorbed energy toward nonradiative rotational relaxation modes rather than fluorescence emission [55–57]. Thus, the pH-dependent fluorescence behavior of CDs can be directly correlated with structural modifications mediated by protonation/deprotonation equilibria.

The synthesized CDs exhibited remarkable dispersibility in aqueous solutions and distinct photoluminescent properties, including excitation-independent and pH-sensitive fluorescence. In contrast to traditional biosensors that rely on biological receptors, which face challenges such as DNA degradation and enzyme instability [58,59], the CDs provide stability and ease of synthesis. Moreover, in the realm of fluorescent probes, optical probes (e.g., organic dyes and fluorescent proteins) are confronted with issues like light source instability and macromolecular binding, which compromise the reliability of the analytical results [52,60]. These advantages underscored the potential of CDs as promising candidates for fluorescent probes and biosensors due to their strong fluorescence, excellent solubility, and non-toxicity [52,61,62].

The economic feasibility of the process is a critical consideration for its broader adoption. Based on the previous economic analysis of XOS production from lignocellulose [63], the total feedstock, reagents, and operating costs amounted approximately to $4.4 per kg. The revenue from the end products, XOS (unit price $35 per kg) and glucose (unit price $5.5 per kg), was $5.22, resulting in a net benefit of $0.82 per kg of TB using the proposed cascade utilization strategy. This process may also contribute to reducing carbon emissions and waste accumulation, supporting the achievement of carbon neutrality [3]. When integrated into a biorefinery framework, lignocellulosic biomass can be efficiently utilized, reducing production costs and enhancing resource efficiency [33]. However, scaling up the proposed process involved several challenges, including optimizing organic acid dosage and increasing XOS yields [64], improving monosaccharide production by adjusting enzyme dosage and increasing substrate concentration for high-solid enzyme hydrolysis [65,66], and improving CDs preparation through better reaction control, precursor consistency, and energy consumption [67,68]. Overcoming these challenges is essential for making this approach both commercially viable and sustainable. In addition to the CDs, future research could explore other high-value products from the TB residues.

The pH adjustment during PA hydrolysis was effective in producing XOS from delignified Toona sinensis branches, yielding a high XOS yield of 61.2 %. This result represented a 1.6-fold improvement over the hydrolysis without pH adjustments and using raw material without delignification. The filtered solid residue from the XOS production was hydrolyzed by cellulase, yielding 87.8 % glucose. Subsequently, the final solid residue was utilized to synthesize CDs, which exhibited excellent dispersibility in aqueous solutions and displayed photoluminescent properties, including excitation-independent and pH-sensitive fluorescence emission behavior. Notably, within the pH range of 1.0 to 9.0, a positive correlation was observed between the fluorescence intensity of the CDs and pH, with a high correlation coefficient (R2 = 0.96). The production of XOS from delignified TB, using optimized hydrolysis conditions, offers a cost-effective approach for generating high-demand prebiotic compounds. Moreover, the successful synthesis of CDs from biomass residues presents a promising avenue for developing fluorescent probes and biosensors, thus contributing to the growing field of biorefinery processes. Overall, this research advances biorefinery technologies and represents a significant step toward developing sustainable and efficient biomass utilization strategies for a greener future.
